notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your household spent $1,870 on childcare and education during the month of May. This total consists of your regular tuition at Bright Horizons Daycare ($1,650) and a single day of backup care through Care.com ($220) that you utilized mid-month.
Childcare and education breakdown
Since these expenses often involve tax credits and specialized education savings, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Primary childcare: $1,870 total spent. This aligns with your specific "childcare & education" category.
Education savings: $300 contributed to Colorado 529 CollegeInvest on May 6. While this is technically a savings transfer rather than a consumption expense, it is a significant part of your monthly kid-related financial commitment.
Health savings: You also made a $400 HSA contribution to HealthEquity on May 8, which can often be used for eligible pediatric healthcare costs.
Items to double-check
To ensure this figure captures your true kid-related spending, I recommend reviewing these three areas where "hidden" expenses often reside:
Mixed-merchant receipts: You spent $736.80 at Costco across two trips ($424.18 and $312.62) and $242.19 at Target on May 21. These are currently categorized as groceries and household, but they likely include diapers, wipes, clothing, or toys that are kid-specific.
Employer reimbursements: Since you used Care.com for backup care ($220), check if your employer (Salesforce or UCHealth) provides a subsidy or reimbursement for this service. If they do, your net cost for May will be lower than the transaction shows.
Tax credit eligibility: For your 2026 planning, keep track of these childcare totals. Depending on your total household income, you may be eligible for the Child and Dependent Care Credit. Additionally, since you are contributing to a 529 plan, you may be eligible for a Colorado state income tax deduction on those contributions.
Would you like me to look back at your March and April spending to see how this May childcare total compares to your typical spring patterns? 💪
